title: Pass Your MOT: Common Light Failures on MOT Explained
metaDescription: Don't let faulty lights fail your MOT! Discover the common light failures on MOT, how to spot them, and simple checks you can do at home. Be prepared and pass easily.
content:
# Pass Your MOT: Common Light Failures on MOT Explained

Faulty car lights are a common reason for MOT failure in the UK. Headlights, brake lights, and indicators must all work correctly and be properly aligned. You can often spot these common light failures on MOTs with simple checks before your test, saving you time and money.

The annual MOT test can feel daunting. But many common failures are easy to prevent. Your car's lighting system is a prime example. Small issues can lead to an instant fail, but they're usually simple to fix.

## Why Your Car's Lights Are Vital for Safety

Car lights are more than just cosmetic. They are critical for road safety, allowing you to see and be seen. Lights communicate your intentions to other drivers, from braking to turning.

Because of this vital role, any defect is serious. An MOT tester will pay close attention to every light. A single blown bulb or a misaligned headlight means an instant fail.

## Common Light Failures on MOT: What to Check

Here's a breakdown of the typical light issues that lead to MOT failures. It's smart to check these yourself before your test.

### 1. Headlights (Dipped & Main Beam)

Your headlights are crucial for night driving and poor visibility. They must work, be the correct colour, and be properly aimed.

*   **Blown Bulbs:** Check both dipped (low beam) and main (high beam) headlights. One often fails before the other.
*   **Misalignment:** Headlights pointing too high can dazzle others. Too low, and they won't light the road properly. This usually needs professional adjustment.
*   **Dull Lenses:** Over time, plastic covers can yellow or haze. This reduces light output significantly. Cleaning kits can help, but severe cases may need new units.
*   **Wrong Colour:** Headlights must emit white or yellow light. Blue-tinted bulbs are usually an illegal modification.

**How to check:** Turn on your lights. Walk around the car. Check all beams work and look clear. Park facing a wall to check alignment.

### 2. Brake Lights

These are often overlooked as you can't see them while driving. Faulty brake lights are a classic MOT failure point.

*   **Not Working:** All three brake lights (including the high-level one) must illuminate. A total failure could be a fuse or brake pedal switch issue.
*   **Only One Working:** This usually points to a single blown bulb.
*   **Intermittent:** A loose connection or faulty switch can cause this. This is tricky to diagnose.

**How to check:** Ask a friend to stand behind your car. Press the brake pedal firmly. Make sure all lights come on brightly. Repeat several times.

### 3. Indicators (Turn Signals)

Indicators tell other drivers where you're going. They must work consistently and be the right colour.

*   **Not Working:** All four indicators (front, side, rear) must flash. The dashboard indicator must also work.
*   **Wrong Flash Rate:** If one bulb blows, the remaining ones on that side often flash much faster. This 'hyper-flashing' is a clear warning sign.
*   **Wrong Colour:** Indicators must flash amber. Any other colour is a fail.

**How to check:** Turn on your hazards, then each indicator in turn. Walk around the car to confirm all flash correctly.

### 4. Fog Lights (Front & Rear)

Front fog lights are not mandatory for an MOT. However, if fitted, they must work. Rear fog lights are mandatory and must work.

*   **Not Working:** If fitted, front fog lights must illuminate. The single rear fog light must work.
*   **Wrong Colour:** Front fogs should be white or yellow. Rear fogs must be red.

**How to check:** Switch on your fog lights. Check they illuminate correctly and are the right colour.

### 5. Number Plate Lights

Often forgotten, but crucial for an MOT pass. Your rear number plate must be illuminated in the dark.

*   **Not Working:** Both lights must work to clearly illuminate the plate.

**How to check:** At dusk or in a dark garage, switch on your dipped headlights. Check both small lights above your rear number plate.

### 6. Reversing Lights

Like front fog lights, reversing lights are not mandatory. But if your car has them, they must work correctly.

*   **Not Working:** If fitted, they must light up when reverse gear is engaged.

**How to check:** With the engine running, engage reverse gear (with the handbrake on!). Ask a friend to check if the white lights at the back come on.

## Frequently Asked Questions About MOT Light Failures

### Can I fail my MOT for a single blown bulb?

Yes, absolutely. Even one single blown bulb in any mandatory lighting system (like headlights, brake lights, or indicators) is an instant MOT failure. It's a quick fix that often prevents a retest.

### How often should I check my car lights?

It's good practice to check all your car's lights at least once a month. Make it part of your routine fluid and tyre checks. Definitely check them thoroughly a week or two before your MOT appointment.

### What if my car fails its MOT on lights?

If your car fails on light issues, get them fixed promptly. You may be able to get a free retest if the repairs are done and the car brought back to the same test centre within 10 working days. For more detailed advice, visit our [Guides](/guides) page.

## Don't Let Simple Light Issues Catch You Out

Checking your car's lights is one of the easiest pre-MOT tasks. A few minutes now can save you the hassle and cost of a retest. Take the time to walk around your vehicle. This simple check can make the difference between a pass and a fail.
